title: Trait member merging: implementation
analysis:
Task 188 builds on the trait conformance infrastructure from task 182 (check_trait_conformance + collect_all_requirements) to implement proper member merging when a structure implements multiple traits.

Current state (task 182): collect_all_requirements already handles requirement dedup by name+type, requirement conflicts (same name, different type → error), default param dedup by name+type, default type conflicts (when not overridden), unlabeled constraint conjunctive composition, and diamond inheritance dedup.

Three gaps remain:

1. **Let default type comparison uses Type::Real sentinel.** In collect_all_requirements (line ~4348), DefaultKind::Let(_) maps to Type::Real for dedup comparison. This means two let defaults with the same name but different types (e.g., Length vs Mass) are incorrectly treated as duplicates instead of producing a type conflict error. Fix: Change DefaultKind::Let(LetDecl) to DefaultKind::Let { cell_type: Type, let_decl: LetDecl } and use actual cell_type for comparison.

2. **No expression comparison for let defaults.** Two let defaults with same name and same type but different expressions are silently merged (second dropped). The task requires: same expression → merge; different expression → error. Fix: Store LetDecl.content_hash in a tracking map; on collision, compare hashes.

3. **Requirements not cross-checked against defaults from other traits.** If trait A requires `param x : Length` (no default) and trait B provides `param x : Length = 10mm` (default), the structure gets a false 'missing required member' error even though a default is available from the merged trait set. Fix: Before reporting missing member, check if all_defaults contains a matching default.

steps.description: Create crates/reify-compiler/tests/trait_merge_tests.rs with helpers (compile_module, compile_first_template) and 2 tests: (a) let_defaults_same_name_same_expr_merge — two traits each with `let area : Real = width * height`, structure implements both, expect 0 errors and exactly 1 'area' value cell. (b) param_requirements_same_name_same_type_merge — two traits each requiring `param x : Length`, structure provides `param x : Length = 5mm`, expect 0 errors (existing behavior, baseline test).

Add failing test: let_defaults_same_name_different_type_error — trait A has `let x : Length = 5mm`, trait B has `let x : Mass = 1kg`, structure implements both. Expect error diagnostic containing 'conflicting'. This will FAIL because DefaultKind::Let uses Type::Real sentinel, making both types appear identical.

Change DefaultKind::Let(LetDecl) to DefaultKind::Let { cell_type: Type, let_decl: LetDecl }. Update compile_trait() to pass the resolved type into the new struct variant. Update all pattern match sites in check_trait_conformance(): (1) pre-registration loop (line ~4178) — use cell_type instead of Type::Real; (2) injection loop (line ~4211) — destructure Let { cell_type, let_decl } and use cell_type for the injected ValueCellDecl. Update collect_all_requirements() — extract cell_type from DefaultKind::Let { cell_type, .. } instead of using Type::Real sentinel. Step-2 test should now pass.

Add failing test: let_defaults_same_name_same_type_different_expr_error — trait A has `let x : Real = width + 1`, trait B has `let x : Real = width * 2`, structure implements both with `param width : Real = 5`. Expect error diagnostic mentioning 'conflicting' and 'expression'. This will FAIL because collect_all_requirements only checks type, not expression content.

Add expression comparison for let defaults in collect_all_requirements. Change seen_defaults from HashMap<String, Type> to HashMap<String, (Type, Option<ContentHash>)>. For DefaultKind::Let, store (cell_type, Some(let_decl.content_hash)). For Param/Constraint, store (type, None). On collision: if both have Some(hash) and hashes differ while types match → emit 'conflicting let expressions' error. Update the signature of seen_defaults and all its access sites. Step-4 test should now pass.

Add failing test: requirement_satisfied_by_cross_trait_default — trait A requires `param x : Length` (no default), trait B provides `param x : Length = 10mm` (default). Structure implements A + B with empty body. Expect 0 errors (the default from B satisfies A's requirement). This will FAIL because check_trait_conformance only checks requirements against structure_members, not against available defaults.

In check_trait_conformance, before the requirement-checking loop (line ~4114), build a HashMap<String, Type> of available default names from all_defaults (excluding constraints). In the None arm of the requirement check (line ~4132), before emitting 'missing required member', check if the requirement name exists in the defaults map with a matching type. If so, skip the error (the default will satisfy the requirement during injection). If the default exists but with a different type, emit a type mismatch error. Step-6 test should now pass.

Add test: let_conflict_resolved_by_structure_override — trait A has `let x : Real = a + 1`, trait B has `let x : Real = a * 2`, structure implements both and provides its own `let x = a + a`. Expect 0 errors (structure override resolves the conflict). Also add: constraints_compose_conjunctively_across_traits — trait A has `constraint x > 0mm`, trait B has `constraint x < 100mm`, structure provides param x, expect both constraints injected (>= 2 constraints).

If step-8 tests fail: For let conflict resolution by override, update collect_all_requirements to check structure_members before reporting let expression conflict (similar to how default type conflicts already check structure_members at line ~4354). For constraint composition, verify existing behavior is correct (should already work via unnamed defaults always-push logic). Fix any remaining issues.

Add comprehensive mixed-merging test: mixed_merging_params_lets_constraints — trait A has `param x : Length`, `let area : Real = x * x`, `constraint x > 0mm`. Trait B has `param x : Length`, `let area : Real = x * x`, `constraint x < 1000mm`. Structure implements A + B with `param x : Length = 5mm`. Expect: 0 errors, exactly 1 'x' value cell, exactly 1 'area' value cell, at least 2 constraints. This validates the complete merging pipeline across member kinds.

design_decisions.decision: Change DefaultKind::Let(LetDecl) to DefaultKind::Let { cell_type: Type, let_decl: LetDecl }
design_decisions.rationale: Mirrors DefaultKind::Param which already stores cell_type. The current sentinel value Type::Real causes all let defaults to appear type-identical during deduplication, masking real type conflicts. Storing the actual resolved type enables correct type comparison.

design_decisions.decision: Use LetDecl.content_hash for expression equivalence comparison
design_decisions.rationale: ContentHash is already computed during parsing for all declarations and captures structural content. Two let declarations with the same name, type annotation, and expression text will have identical content_hash. This is conservative (whitespace-sensitive) but correct and zero-cost since hashes are pre-computed. No need to implement PartialEq on Expr or add new hash infrastructure.

Adding Option<ContentHash> to the tracking map enables expression comparison for let defaults without adding a separate data structure. None for param/constraint defaults (expression comparison not needed), Some(hash) for let defaults. Minimal change to existing dedup logic.

design_decisions.decision: Cross-check requirements against available defaults before reporting missing member
design_decisions.rationale: When trait A requires param x and trait B provides default param x (same type), the merged view should see x as available. Without this cross-check, the structure gets a false 'missing required member' error for x even though it will be injected by B's default. This is the semantic heart of 'merge members across all traits'.

design_decisions.decision: Do not check for param-vs-let kind conflicts (same name, different member kind across traits)
design_decisions.rationale: A param (user-settable) and a let (computed) with the same name from different traits represent a semantic conflict, but the task description does not explicitly require this check. The current infrastructure tracks requirements and defaults in separate maps without kind discrimination. Adding kind-conflict detection would require broader refactoring. Deferred to a future task.

design_decisions.decision: New test file trait_merge_tests.rs (not appending to trait_conformance_tests.rs)
design_decisions.rationale: Follows project convention of separate test files per feature area. Merging across multiple traits is a distinct concern from basic single-trait conformance checking. Keeps trait_conformance_tests.rs focused on its current scope.

reuse.how: Existing recursive function for traversing refinement chains with diamond dedup. Extend its seen_defaults map and add expression comparison — no structural changes needed.
Existing function that checks requirements and injects defaults. Add cross-check of requirements against defaults, update DefaultKind::Let pattern matches.
Already computed during parsing for every LetDecl. Compare content_hash values to detect expression differences between let defaults with same name and type.
Same helper pattern (parse + compile + extract) used across all 24 compiler test files. Copy into new trait_merge_tests.rs.
Standard pattern: filter diagnostics by Severity::Error, assert count, check message contains expected substring. Used throughout all test files.
Existing builder pattern for creating error diagnostics with labeled source spans. Used for new error messages (conflicting let expressions, cross-trait type mismatch).
